name: Number of partnerships
explanation: Through careful filtering of the research set, we identified and counted 53 unique organizations with official AI-related partnerships with Telecom Italia S.p.A. Duplicates were resolved at the organizational level (e.g., Poste Italiane, Google Cloud) and only partnerships explicitly related to AI—as confirmed by both 'ai_relation_check' and direct company involvement—were included. Partnerships only loosely mentioning AI without implementation details, or with ambiguous company attribution, were excluded from the count.

explanation: To break down the 84 confirmed AI talents: 38 are in AI & ML Engineering (roles like Machine Learning Engineer, Senior Data Scientist, AI Application Engineer, etc.); 26 are in Data & Analytics (Data Engineer, Data Scientist, Data Analyst, etc.); 18 are in NLP & Generative AI (NLP Engineer, Generative AI Expert, or with direct involvement in GenAI/NLP projects); and 2 are in AI Product & Strategy (leaders directly involved in AI strategy and adoption). name: Formal governance structure
answer: Board-approved Artificial Intelligence Guidelines define TIM’s organizational and governance responsibilities for developing, using, and procuring AI systems across the Group. Oversight is exercised by the Board of Directors (supported by the Control & Risks and Sustainability Committees), with AI-related risks integrated into the Enterprise Risk Management process and monitored via the ERM Steering Committee. Implementation is coordinated through a centralized, cross‑functional team, and the Guidelines are continuously monitored to ensure regulatory compliance for all Group entities and relevant third parties.
explanation:
- The Board of Directors approved TIM’s Artificial Intelligence Guidelines and ensures alignment with company strategy [2].
- The AI Guidelines define the organizational and governance structure for managing AI issues (development, use, and purchase) [1].
- The Guidelines apply Group-wide to corporate bodies, management, employees, external workers, and certain third parties [3].
- Oversight mechanisms include the Board supported by its committees (Control & Risks, Sustainability) [2].
- AI-related risks are included among key risk factors and are handled within the ERM framework; the ERM Steering Committee meets to monitor risk profile and tolerances [2].
- TIM has adopted centralized governance for AI by establishing a multifunctional, cross‑functional team; the Guidelines are constantly monitored to ensure regulatory compliance and incorporate regulatory changes [1].
definition: Oversight mechanisms and committees for AI strategy, compliance, and lifecycle

name: AI Ethics documentation
answer: TIM has formal Artificial Intelligence Guidelines, approved by the Board of Directors, that translate ethical principles into operational requirements for the development, use, and procurement of AI systems. The Guidelines define ethical principles, the internal/external regulatory framework (e.g., EU Charter, EU AI Act, GDPR/e‑Privacy, UNESCO AI ethics, EC “Ethics by Design”), and the organizational and governance structure for managing AI. They apply to corporate bodies, management, all employees, external workers and, where required, third‑party partners, are monitored/updated for regulatory changes, and policies are made available via the Group intranet/website.
explanation: Claims state TIM has Board‑approved Artificial Intelligence Guidelines that set the regulatory framework and translate ethical principles into day‑to‑day operations, including ethical principles, regulatory references, and governance (approval by Board; framework and content) [1][3]. The Guidelines explicitly reference EU Charter, EU AI Act, GDPR/e‑Privacy, UNESCO AI ethics, and the EC “Ethics by Design” guidance [1]. Scope covers corporate bodies, employees, external workers, and, where required, third parties [7]. Ongoing monitoring and updates for regulatory changes are described [5]. TIM makes policies available on its intranet/website to share contents [1]. Additional communications align with EU “Trustworthy AI” ethics [3].
definition: Existence and scope of formal AI ethics guidelines and documentation

explanation: Based on the competency matrices and director profiles disclosed in Telecom Italia S.p.A.'s 2024 annual report and investor documentation, there are no individuals currently serving on the Board of Directors or Supervisory Board with proven AI expertise. The most technology-oriented director, Domitilla Benigni, has a background in cybersecurity, not artificial intelligence. Other directors list general digital technology skills but lack evidence of AI-specific experience. This is explicitly stated in the relations data and confirmed by the analysis of board member backgrounds.

name: Number of patents since 2020
explanation:
All company results are based on a uniform WIPO PATENTSCOPE query identifying AI-related patents filed or granted since 1 Jan 2020.
The search was performed globally using the same parameters for all 40 companies.
Each query targeted group- or holding-level names and filtered for AI-relevant terms in titles, abstracts, claims, and descriptions.

Generic query used: ALLNAMES:("<<Company Name(s)>>") AND AD:[01.01.2020 TO "*"] AND EN_DE:("AI" OR "ML" OR "DL" OR "artificial intelligence" OR "machine learning" OR "deep learning" OR "neural network" OR "transformers" OR "reinforcement learning" OR "predictive model" OR "federated learning" OR "natural language processing" OR "NLP" OR "chatbot" OR "fraud detection" OR "recommendation system")
definition: Total patents granted or filed by the company related to AI technologies since 2020

name: First year of AI deployment
answer: 2023 — Telecom Italia (TIM) deployed an AI algorithm on its 5G/edge computing platform to automatically select and compile real-time video highlights for the Giro d’Italia U23 cycling race, representing the company’s first publicly documented at-scale AI use in a live production setting.
explanation: The Giro d’Italia U23 project is the earliest externally documented instance in which TIM placed an AI system into live, operational use. Announced and executed in 2023, the solution ingested multiple live camera feeds at the network edge and, through machine-learning models, generated highlight clips that were immediately delivered to the race’s mobile application users. No verifiable sources describe an earlier production-scale AI deployment by TIM, while later projects—such as the 2024 Google-powered customer-service voice agent and the 2025 Sparkle AISNA network-assurance platform—occur after 2023. Therefore 2023 is the correct first-deployment year.
definition: The first publicly documented year in which the company implemented or deployed AI systems at operational scale (e.g., network automation, customer service bots, predictive maintenance)
output_variable: Provide the Year and details on the depoloyment

name: workplace reduction in FTEs
answer: No explicit % or FTE reduction figure reported; only indicative efficiency gains (e.g., 3,700 staff hours/year saved at Sparkle, ~2 FTEs), but no formal headcount reduction attributed directly to AI/automation.
explanation: Analysis of public disclosures and internal reports for Telecom Italia S.p.A. reveals that while the company has achieved measurable efficiency gains through AI (e.g., Sparkle's AISNA project saved 3,700 staff hours/year, roughly equivalent to 2 FTEs), there is no formal, company-wide reporting of workforce reduction (either as a % or absolute FTEs) explicitly attributed to automation or AI adoption. Larger workforce reductions at TIM (e.g., from 47,180 to 26,900 employees in 2023-2024) are primarily due to structural changes (NetCo divestiture), not directly to AI. Strategic plans reference AI-driven efficiency, but reductions are achieved via natural attrition and voluntary exits, not explicit AI-linked layoffs. Thus, no concrete % or FTE reduction can be attributed solely to AI/automation based on available evidence.
definition: Reported workforce reduction (absolute or % change in Full-Time Equivalents) explicitly attributed to automation or AI adoption.
output_variable: Provide an indicative % number or FTE indication

name: Release schedule
explanation: TIM does not publish an official AI release calendar, yet publicly available evidence shows it introducing multiple AI features within individual years. In 2023 alone it launched several customer-facing AI capabilities (virtual assistant upgrades, real-time voice/SMS transcription with call-summary, and the Giro d’Italia U23 edge-video AI). Additional large-scale rollouts followed in 2024 (Google-powered voice agent for customer care) and 2025 (Perplexity Pro knowledge-assistant offer for mobile subscribers). Collectively, this pattern supports an average cadence of roughly two to three significant AI releases each year.
definition: Frequency or cadence of AI-related product or model releases (e.g., quarterly, annual, continuous).
output_variable: Provide single metrics as an output

name: Number of data breaches in past 5 years
explanation: TIM S.p.A. reports the following "customer data leaks, thefts or losses" (GRI 418-1): 2 incidents in 2023, 1 in 2022, and 0 in 2021 [1]. For 2024, disclosures state that although there was one medium-impact DDoS and two low-impact incidents, there were no data losses or compromise (i.e., zero data-breach cases) [2]. Data for 2020 is not provided in the claims. Summing the reported years within the last five-year window (2021–2024) yields 3 data-breach incidents.
raw_data_point: Number of data breaches in past 5 years
definition: number of data breaches
output_variable: Statethe number of incidents related to data confidentiality, data breach, or any other data security issues in past 5 years

name: AI ethics governance model yes or no
answer: Yes — TIM has Board-approved Artificial Intelligence Guidelines that set ethical principles and define an organizational and governance structure for managing AI issues; a centralized cross‑functional team oversees AI risks, and the guidelines are monitored and updated to align with external frameworks such as the EU AI Act and UNESCO recommendations.
explanation: TIM’s Board approved Group Artificial Intelligence Guidelines that define the regulatory, organizational and governance framework for AI across the Group [1]. TIM also adopted centralized governance via a multifunctional, cross‑functional team to manage AI-related risks (privacy, discrimination, regulatory compliance, security) [5]. The AI Guidelines reference external standards and regulations including the EU AI Act, GDPR, e‑Privacy Directive, and UNESCO’s AI ethics recommendation [2], and are subject to constant monitoring and updates to maintain regulatory compliance [3].

output_variable: Provide Yes or no and add additional context

Sparkle’s AISNA and the Giro d’Italia highlight-selection engine describe benefits qualitatively but publish no numbers, so they are excluded from the quantitative-impact tally, keeping the count at two.

name: Number of AI use cases with financial impact since 2023
explanation: Three of the four identified AI projects explicitly link to economic outcomes: • Google voice agent – 20 % efficiency improvement translates into lower OPEX per handled call; • TIMFin digital lending – 437 k device-financing contracts and ~€33 m personal-loan volume demonstrate direct revenue generation; • Sparkle AISNA – corporate communication emphasises OPEX savings from automated troubleshooting and faster fault resolution. The Giro d’Italia highlight engine is designed for fan engagement and carries no published income or cost figures, so it is excluded. Accordingly, the number of financially impactful AI use cases equals three, which is a logical subset of the four total use cases.

name: Number of AI talents
explanation: We identified 84 unique employees currently holding roles at Telecom Italia S.p.A. (TIM) or its direct subsidiaries, whose titles and/or job descriptions align with AI Development, Data Engineering, AI Software Implementation, or AI Product Management. Each entry was validated to ensure the individual was currently employed, in scope, and not duplicated, with ambiguous, former, or non-AI-focused roles excluded. This count is based strictly on evidence from organizational sources, company listings, and role definitions [TIM LinkedIn, company website, TheOrg, SAS customer stories, ResearchGate, RocketReach, Gruppo TIM newsroom, SAS.com, TheOrg.com, LinkedIn].
definition: Number of employees in AI roles with the definition: AI Development,  Data Engineering, AI Software Implementation, and AI Product Management.
